subtopics.summary: Students observe pictures of various vehicles like bullock carts, motorcycles, buses, auto-rickshaws, scooters, bicycles, vans, and cars.

subtopics.name: Vehicles by Distance
subtopics.summary: Discusses how different vehicles are used for nearby, far away, and much distant places, mentioning specific examples like cycle, auto, rickshaw, boat, horse cart, jeep, aeroplane, and ships.

subtopics.name: Travel Challenges in Rural Areas
subtopics.summary: Presents scenarios of travel to villages without roads or bus facilities, and for special occasions like marriages, highlighting the need for alternative transport.

subtopics.name: Animal-Powered Transport
subtopics.summary: Introduces animals like bulls, horses, elephants, donkeys, and camels used for pulling carts or carrying goods.

subtopics.name: Changes in Transport Methods
subtopics.summary: Discusses the decrease in the use of bullock-carts and horse-pulled carts and the reasons behind it, alongside the use of modern vehicles for events and migration.

subtopics.name: Dangers of Overcrowding
subtopics.summary: Students observe pictures of overcrowded vehicles and discuss the risks associated with such travel.

subtopics.name: Importance of Safe Travel
subtopics.summary: Concludes that overcrowded travel is dangerous and should be avoided.

subtopics.name: Past vs. Present Vehicles
subtopics.summary: Encourages students to compare vehicles from the past with those of the present and discuss with elders.

subtopics.name: Key Vocabulary
subtopics.summary: Lists important terms introduced in the chapter related to transport.

subtopics.name: What have we learnt?
subtopics.summary: Summarizes the main learning outcomes of the chapter, including different vehicles, modes of transport, animal transport, and safe travel practices.

subtopics.name: Artists
subtopics.summary: Lists the artists who contributed to the textbook, including their names, designations, and districts.

subtopics.name: Textbook Publication Team
subtopics.summary: Lists the key personnel involved in the textbook's publication, including the Director of the State Council for Educational Research and Training, and the Professor & Head of the Department of Curriculum and Text Books Development, SCERT.

subtopics.name: QR CODE TEAM
subtopics.summary: Identifies the QR Code Team and provides two QR codes with their respective IDs (M2J3W7 and D4G2L3), along with the Telugu title for the team.

Think, to whom the plants you have planted will be useful.
Key words: 1. uses of trees 2. adoption of plants 3. plants grown in houses 4. desert plants 5. water plants 6. plants grown in open places

What have we learnt? Different plants grow in our localities. The plants around us are different. Some grow very tall. Some are very short. Some are small in size. Trees like neem grow bigger with branches. Trees like palm grow taller without branches. There are many uses of trees. We get flowers, fruits and wood from trees. Some trees do not grow in our surroundings. But we use the flowers, fruits and wood of these trees. We plant saplings in our school, in the premises of our house and as many places as possible and protect them. The plants like cactus, aloevera grow in sandy soils. These are called desert plants.

Do you know? Leaves of the trees such as almond, mango etc. change their colour as they age.
Do you know? The smoke that comes out due to burning of the fallen leaves is injurious to health. Instead of burning, the fallen leaves should be dumped into a compost pit. Don't burn them.

Do you know? All vegetables should be cooked only after washing thoroughly. Nutrients are lost if vegetables are washed after chopping. Rice should not be washed a lot before cooking. Water should not be thrown after the rice is cooked, if we do this all nutrients would be lost. Our everyday meals should contain pulses and leafy vegetables. Vegetables should not be over cooked. Carrots, beetroot, radish, cucumber, onion, coriander leaves, mint (pudina) etc. can be eaten raw.
Key words 1. food 2. cooked food 3. raw food 4. digestion 5. cooking utensils 6. nutrients

What have we learnt? Food gives us energy. We get food from plants and animals. We eat some food items cooked while some are eaten raw. Food gets tastier when it is cooked. Cooked food is easily digested. A food item will not be cooked by all in the same manner. Nowadays cookers, ovens etc., are being used for cooking. All food items are not cooked in the same way. The food items are cooked either by heating, boiling or frying. Different cooking utensils are required to cook food. Vegetables should be washed thoroughly before cooking.

What have we learnt? • The food habits of a region depend on crops grown and the avail-ability of food items. • Special dishes are prepared during festivals and marriages. • Animals and birds also need food. They have different kinds of food habits. • All members of the family should eat together. This helps in the distribution of food items equally among all the members. We feel happy when we eat together. • Food habits change according to age. • We should wash our hands and legs with soap before eating.

When the roofs are slanting the rain water flows down quickly.

Key words 1. journey 2. modes of transport 3. vehicles 4. air transport 5. vehicles that sail on water 6. vehicles that run on the land 7. animals used for travel 8. accidents while travelling 9. modes of transport in olden days

What have we learnt? People use different vehicles to travel from one place to another. Different modes of transport are used for journeys. Animals like bull, camel, donkey, horse, elephant are also used as different means of transport. Different vehicles are used depending on the distance to be travelled. Travelling in heavily packed vehicles with excess travellers is dangerous. Travelling in private vehicles like tractors, jeeps, lorries should be avoided.
